title: Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models
summary: Discovered that language model performance follows predictable power-law relationships with model size, dataset size, and compute budget. The key finding: performance improves smoothly and predictably as you scale up, with no signs of plateauing. This paper provided the theoretical justification for the massive scaling investments that followed — if you could predict that spending 10x more compute would yield a specific improvement, the ROI became calculable.
key_contributions: Discovered power-law scaling relationships for neural language models
Showed that model size matters more than dataset size for a fixed compute budget
Demonstrated smooth, predictable improvement with scale — no plateaus observed
Provided the quantitative basis for the multi-billion dollar scaling race
impact: Transformed AI research from an art into a more predictable science. Justified the massive compute investments by OpenAI, Google, Anthropic, and Meta. Made the 'bigger is better' hypothesis empirically testable.
query_hooks: Why do AI companies need so much compute?
Does bigger always mean better in AI?
Can you predict AI model performance?
